speaker: interviewer
text: --is Louis Hofman, H-O-F-M-A-N. This is roll five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hofman, you jumped out from the shul window, and you are on the way to Konstantynow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Konstantynow because I didn't have the guts to go back to Miedzyrzec. I knew I have a few people from my family-- nieces and a nephew. What I couldn't-- I make my decision to go to Konstantynow. It was very hard to go there. I almost lost my life there. But I came back a few days later. I went in under that attic, where I left them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Left whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: The nieces, with my nephew. They all had frozen feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of your nieces? Do you remember? What is the name of the niece that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was Esther Hofman-- no, Silverman. Chana and Esther Goldring, Moishe [INAUDIBLE]. Rosin-- oh, my God. [? Rosinger-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they still alive today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Chana is alive. She survived on the [INAUDIBLE]. She was in Auschwitz. She was all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me what happened when you found them with the frozen feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. They were crying. They were happy to see me back, because they was outlaws. But they opened up the ghetto two days later. They were in the ghetto. They were sitting all winter till Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943, in a small room, very bad, no food. But I had to go every couple days to buy, sell something, [NON-ENGLISH], go to outside of the ghetto and bring in a piece of bread or potatoes. And that's it. So you were helpful to supply food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a father from five children. There was nobody to supply to them. There was no help. What happened then? And what was the situation in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto was-- they was predicting-- that time, they was predicting [INAUDIBLE] maybe they're going to leave us. Maybe not. Maybe this, maybe that-- all kind of stories. It was typhus, sickness. It was destruction, everything, you name it. What everybody was thinking, how can I get such a luxury [INAUDIBLE] [? death ?], like [INAUDIBLE], or the [INAUDIBLE]. I cannot-- [INAUDIBLE] I cannot die. They called this [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now it's 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 1943, the new in ghetto Warsaw is the [NON-ENGLISH], the uprise. In that, we were [? shaking. ?] Maybe today, maybe tomorrow, they're going to [INAUDIBLE] us. This happened on May 2, on a Sunday morning. This was [NON-ENGLISH]. the [NON-ENGLISH], from all the Jews from the world. And of Saturday night, we heard [INAUDIBLE] talking between each other. And the ghetto is surrounded with Gestapo-- all kind of tsuris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What police, the Yiddische police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddische. Then we start to [INAUDIBLE] ourselves. We had a bunker in the house. But everybody cannot fit in. So I took my-- I left to go in my brother-in-law, my two nieces-- they went in, in the bunker. I and my niece-- one niece and the nephew, we went on the attic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The daylight, they took us down from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapo and [NON-ENGLISH]. They didn't took us down by ladder. No, they threw us down. We get in to the unschlagplatz. No, we went to the unschlagplatz, and we came at the platz sitting on the knees, about 2,000 people already. I came, was maybe 1,000, but slowly, they're going to come in, They come in, they're shooting and they're knocking. And they-- it's hard to describe.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they're beating?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're beating and they're knocking, and they're doing all the kind of [? tsuris. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have dogs? No. That time-- maybe they have. I didn't see it. I was-- what happened? We start to organize, me and another boy, a mishpocha, my family, a [? white ?] family, but a friend, Shmuel Goldring. We want to send messages around. Maybe shall we start to-- to make a riot. The answer was from the people. We don't want it, because I was three times jumped to the train. I broke my arm, and I [INAUDIBLE] family. My wife is not alive. My children-- Who said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people. You cannot blame it, because there was a run. It was a very solid police, and the Gestapo was watching us very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were a lot of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because [NON-ENGLISH], the uprise was in Warsaw. And they was [? watching-- ?] they was thinking maybe this is going to be also.

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you? About 2 o'clock, they took us to the train, five people in the row, armed. And they walked with us. They pushed us in, in the train. That time, in the train, we came in. My niece was separate. My nephew was with me. A cousin was with me. The son was with me. And we came in. And the window was closed with metal-- not with wire. No more wire-- a small window and a metal plate, with heavy bolts. And we got this-- we got-- we were thinking, how can we do this? How many people in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that train was, they expect--

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 60, 70 people in the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: The cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was prepared for more people, but a lot of them was hiding. They didn't come out, all of them, to the-- to the transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the train-- me, and my nephew, my cousin, my friend, and another 60, 70 people. When we came in, we got disappointed. It's no wire by the windows. It's metal. One guy was so strong, like a Superman. He broke up the metal bolts. And one guy, his brother said, I'm going to jump out. I'm going to try to open the door. He climbed out through the window, and he start to dig and dig and dig. And he said, I can't do nothing. Then [INAUDIBLE] told him jump. And he jumped out. Then some other man, from [NON-ENGLISH], he was with us. He said, let me go. He went out, and they start to-- and the train is going fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: While the train is running?

speaker: interviewee
text: Got an express train to [INAUDIBLE] Jewish people, the people jumping out. They want to-- what happened? They tried. He went. He was digging, and digging. And inside was a man without-- with a crutch. He had without a leg to the knee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside the train with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he said to him, give me your crutch. I want it to help me up and out. He gave him. He said, but give me back. Then I thought he's going to go-- and it is to go to the crematorium. He jumped out too, [INAUDIBLE] the train. He came maybe 50 kilometers with that crutch. I saw him later, a week or two weeks later, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? He opened up. Then he said, they shoot him in the arm, They said jump. Did you jump too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also jump?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait. I'll come to it. So what happened? We took inside, with [INAUDIBLE]. because he could not do anything. [INAUDIBLE] They'd shoot him in the arm. Then we opened up, and my friend was the third jumper, and I was the fourth. I jumped out from the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody guarding the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of them jumped out wrong. They went in, into the train. I jumped to the locomotive. When I jumped out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are any Gestapo guarding the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he outside, on top?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the top was sitting, and the shooting, was started-- unbelievable. Like-- I don't know what to say. Constantly tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, and so forth. Then I jumped out. Luckily, I was lucky. I scratched just my hand and ear a little bit. But then-- and I was rolling myself, and make myself dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? When the train left, I stand up. I am alive. And I start slowly to go to the woods. Polish kids-- 20 years, 22-- they grabbed me, three of them. They want to take me to the Gestapo. And I said, hey, you're mistaken. This is not the guy you're going to take in. I have nothing to lose. They're staying, and I'm staying. They stay. I cannot go away. They're not going away, and I don't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I played dummy. I stay. I'm not going to run away. All of a sudden, they got four more. There's seven. And one guy said to me, I'm not a plain man. I am a [NON-ENGLISH]. Even one didn't say leave him alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened? They took me away-- my clothes, which was worthless, [NON-ENGLISH], with lice. They took me away my watch, my few zlotys, and they let me go. I went in, in the woods. I was sitting. How did you go? Did you have any clothes left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got on a tee shirt. I wearing a tee shirt. I was freezing to death. It was a cold night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened? This was near the station, between Miedzyrzec and Lukov-- [NON-ENGLISH]. This was the town for the station.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I picked up myself. I was staying in the woods till it gets dark. Dark, a couple people-- I hope somebody is walking at night. I saw the people. I recognized them. They recognized me. I was the leader, to go close to Miedzyrzec.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came up a couple miles before Miedzyrzec. We went in in the woods. The neighbors of that-- a small town a few hours is a summer place, [? Yelnitsa. ?] And we were sitting there. Nobody-- nothing to eat, not to drink. And I have not even one penny. I said, who has money? Give me. I'll get bread and milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me. I went in. All of a sudden, we hear--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the houses, to the farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: To a farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I got milk and bread. I paid him, because I didn't have money. I got from the few people who they went with me. In the meantime, we are shooting. A train is going [? shooting. ?] It was another transport. This was-- we went Sunday, and they went Monday. On Sunday, we were sitting on the knees, Polish people, with the kids, with the corsages, they went to the church, and they was laughing of us. This was boiling me. I couldn't take it. They go in with the sidewalk to the church, and laughing, the kids with the corsages. And we're sitting on the knees going [INAUDIBLE] to be slaughtered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, when you jumped to get back, when you jumped out from the train, were you the only one that was left alive? What happened to the other people that were--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, was a lot of survivors from the jumping. But the war took so long, they got killed, there, and there, and there, and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you jumped out, nobody else left with you? You were the only one?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I left only, but after me, was a lot [? of it ?] was jumping.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where the train was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Treblinka. I know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: All my family went to Treblinka. And I'll tell you a story. When I was hesitating, shall I jump or not, but maybe I'll break a leg. What I'm going to do? Or maybe I'll be injured. But in the meantime, around the 10th kilometer, I got wild. I said, maybe let me take a chance. What's going to be the outcome? I became a wild man. And I jumped out, luckily.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened now, when you were in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to my neighborhood. And I was sitting by a Polack. And I wanted to go see what's doing. A friend of mine, he has a bunker by a Polish family. I met him, and he said his brother, my friend-- he was the third jumper-- he is alive. He came. Then I went with him for a few days. I was sitting in the bunker, by them. And I went back to Miedzyrzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the bunker in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Farmhouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: By a farmer, in a barn. In a barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the bunker built?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the bunker built?

speaker: interviewee
text: Digged out and covered up with-- [NON-ENGLISH] was on top. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And straw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- I don't know. Was digged out of a hole and put a ceiling with boards, and put up the dirt from the [NON-ENGLISH]. And this was a small hole to go in, covered from a [? mask, ?] and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, at that time it was two-- started with four-- with four. And they winded up with seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, three days. I went back to Miedzyrzec. Maybe anybody from my family survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was May the 2, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this I was in the woods, and I had no place--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, I'll tell you. I was-- I was there. It was no place. A big world, and for me is all things small. I have no place to go. I went to a farmer, and the wife was fighting with him, and I didn't want to make a bad life between the two of them account of me, and I was-- to make a long story short--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wait. I went back to Miedzyrzec. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --this was July the 16th. A couple of people from my town, they asked me, Louie, take me to [INAUDIBLE]. And I know a farm. I will go there. And I said yes. In the meantime, this was-- and they was thinking-- I don't know. Then later, I said, I'm not doing the right thing. But I said, I'm going to keep my word.

speaker: interviewer
text: On June 6-- July the 16th, June or July-- July the 16th, what happened? We supposed to, at night, go out from Miedzyrzec ghetto-- about 10 people. Around 11 o'clock, we hear how the Jewish partisans killed two-- three [NON-ENGLISH]. And then [? right ?] [? away ?] they said, are they Jews? And we was surrounded, the whole ghetto. Then we started to run away. I was running, and the police was running near me, over me. Were you running alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not. Three people was following me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you run to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was waiting outside-- outside already, past the ghetto, past the town. And I was waiting for the rest of the people. They didn't come. I was waiting and waiting and waiting and waiting. Then I went to a farmer. This was Friday night. Saturday morning, people came from there. They said to me, everyone got killed on the spot. No more transports. Forget it. So what happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go to the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple hours later-- my wife today, she was that time with another girl. She came to that farmer, and to tell my mother and the other woman, the [? landslady, ?] she [? sits ?] there, and there, and there, and there. At night, I had to go to them. And it was terrible to go near that village, because there they killed every Jew what they saw there. I went there in the woods, and I got [? them ?] We came back to a farmer--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's mother, and my landslady, and another few people. I got them out from there, and I got-- I went to that farmer. And that farmer was one Jew. He said, dear Louie, take the people and go. Don't stay here, because you're going be in trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where you were by this farmer in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not by this farmer. She was a lady, a widow with kids. She gave [INAUDIBLE]-- for a couple zlotys, she let you stay there, in a barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went near Konstantynow. On the way, we had to stop, because the daylight. We had to sit in the field. In the truth, I was sorry what I [? pent up ?] myself with such a-- such a problem. Maybe they would survive. I'm taking them probably on a [? losing ?] way. OK, what are you going to do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said-- I came to my neighborhood. And I went in, in the woods, and they were sitting. And there was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go into the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Konstantynow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- where were you sitting? Did you find some people there in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we took-- we was a group of 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: You were a-- and where did you go in the woods? In the woods, not far from Konstantynow. And we were sitting there. And I see it's bad, a day, two, three days, four days. We had a Polish guy. I have his name-- Wasla Shpura. He was the messenger. He was the connection guy. He knew everybody very easily. He was-- we paid him. But he was good. And he was-- he was not by himself. So what happened? He said, the only one way to do-- go in that section. I'll take you there. And go at night. In the morning, I'll come out, And I'm going to call the dog's name. Then you come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is a cemetery-- prisoners, Russian prisoners, was killed there-- about six, seven. The bones is all around. There were Jews-- the Polish people were afraid to go there. Then you got to make a bunker and stay there. This is what we did. We took out the people from the woods, go back in the other forest, and we made--

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the cemetery you made the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cemetery was from bones, was [INAUDIBLE] skeletons, from the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you made the bunker there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We made it. We made it-- me and a few of them. We did this. And there we were sitting. But we was not enough. We made maybe 10 more bunkers. If this was [? built ?] up, then we go to the other one. And this was-- we're sitting from June 16 or July 16 till July the 30th, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me please, you said you made 10 bunkers. They were all around the same area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the same area. It was the wider sections-- other sections.

speaker: interviewer
text: But around Konstantynow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Around Konstantynow. The woods-- the way-- the woods neighborhood was called [? Walima-- ?] [? Walima. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a few-- [INAUDIBLE], but he was baking bread in night time, we should pick up. We paid him. And potatoes, we were stealing. Water we had to steal. We had to go far, far for water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about 60 people-- and 50. And six people got killed nine days before the liberation. One man--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] got killed-- the [? AK ?] was in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: The [? AK? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]. The code word.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? AK ?] is the--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] . And they caught one guy there, and they took him to the other place, to show where the other one, and they killed him-- six people. And one guy, he was twice in Treblinka. He struggled to go out into the [NON-ENGLISH]. And he escaped. And these Polish people killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I have a list-- a few-- [INAUDIBLE]. What-- what-- how was the life? How did you conduct yourself all throughout the time that you were in that bunker with the 10 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was divided.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in several bunkers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were separate-- five in one place, five in the other place, because we didn't want to put in the [? cheese ?] in one cake.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and there were other people with you, who provided the food for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, was another guy. He was trying to help out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people keep themselves busy?

speaker: interviewee
text: What busy?

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside the bunker, what was it? You were sitting--

speaker: interviewee
text: We was laying like dead people, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what the bunker looked like inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside it was looking-- a very small place for six people. And the mattress was from branches from the birch leaves. And if you have to go, for example, to make a pee pee, you had to wake up the other one. You couldn't go out. We was-- [CLAPS].

speaker: interviewer
text: Very close quarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. It was a-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in 1944, July 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we was-- we came to our city, Konstantynow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. The Russian liberates us, the Russians, in July the 30th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- how did you hear that you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was [INAUDIBLE] for a couple days by a Polish family. But the other ones in--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- while you were in the bunker, the people, what did the people talk about? Did they take talk about the future, what's going to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know the Germans are going to break their necks. They're going to lose. But we-- but we are going to survive. This what was our talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any kind of newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had connections with the news, from the radio, from the Polish peoples, because I picked up [? one ?] of [? five ?] from that, when I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how did you hear about the Russians approaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was in that time in a Polish family, also in a bunker. But when I heard, I looked out a little hole. I saw the Russian people. And the housewife, the Polish, she told us already. They're coming in. They're already not far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the Russians came, what were your thoughts? And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians come? We left the woods, the people. We went into the city. It was very, very hard to step into that town-- very hard. You see your house, your brother's house, with the children, with your family, and to see the people, which they belong there, and they're laughing of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean other people were in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people, the Polacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish people. They was wondering, where were you? We was looking for you. I know you was looking-- to kill. One fellow told me, a Polish fellow, Louie, watch yourself, [? for ?] they're all your friends, because they want to kill you even now. And it's not Germans they're going to kill you. They killed a lot of Jews after the war, when the Russians-- when the Germans was not there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to reload again.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we will continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.
